en.extract:
The hooded parrot (Psephotellus dissimilis) is a species of parrot native to the Northern Territory in Australia. It is found in savannah and open woodland and is one of two extant species in its genus that breed in termite mounds. It has declined from much of its original range.


Taxonomy
One of three species known as antbed parrots, the hooded parrot is closely related to (and sometimes considered a subspecies of) the golden-shouldered parrot (Psephotellus chrysopterygius). A genetic study revealed its ancestors most likely diverged from ancestors of the latter species in the late Miocene or early Pliocene at the conclusion of the 'Hill Gap'.
Norwegian naturalist Robert Collett described the hooded parrot in 1898. Its species name is the Latin word dissimilis "different" and either refers to its sexual dimorphism or its different appearance from its closest relative. Black-hooded parrot is an alternative name.


Description
The hooded parrot is a medium-sized parrot, up to 26 cm (10 in) long, weighing 50 to 60 g (1.8 to 2.1 oz). The male has a black head, bluish-grey beak, dark-brown irises, and its wings have a yellow patch. Its upper-parts are brown, its lower parts are turquoise blue, its back is brown, the tail is olive green tipped pale blue, and its legs are pink-grey. The female is a dull-green parrot with a grey-brown head and pink undertail coverts. Juveniles are similar to the female.
The male hooded parrot can be distinguished from the similar looking male golden-shouldered parrot mainly by the absence of a pale frontal band, a larger yellow patch on the wings, and absence of the red on the abdomen. The female is very similar to the female Yellow-shouldered parrot being identified by the absence of a pale frontal band and an absence of red on the abdomen.


Distribution and habitat
An Australian endemic, the hooded parrot inhabits to semi-arid areas of northeast Northern Territory, and is separated from the golden-shouldered parrot by the Carpentarian faunal barrier.
Though this species has disappeared from most of its original range, it remains common in protected areas. The hooded parrot is evaluated as Least Concern on IUCN Red List of Threatened Species.


Behaviour

The diet consists mainly of seeds, berries and vegetables.
The hooded parrot digs cavities in termite mounds. It generally chooses conical termite mounds, only successful in flat two dimensional mounds if it digs at the edge rather than on the face of the mound. Excavation takes around three weeks. The tunnel is roughly horizontal and around 50 to 80 cm (20 to 31 in) long.
Breeding takes place once or twice a year between April and August. The female lays four or five white, slightly shiny eggs that are 21 mm (0.83 in) long by 19 mm (0.75 in) wide.
The hooded parrot has a close ecological relationship with the moth species Trisyntopa neossophila. The female moth lays eggs in the parrot nesting cavity, so that the eggs hatch at the same time as the baby hooded parrots. The moth larvae live in small tunnels at the base of the cavity and emerge to eat the dark material of parrot feces, leaving the white uric acid deposits. They build a silk layer across the base of the cavity to protect themselves from chicks. They pupate in the cavity walls once the juvenile parrots leave the nest.

The goanna species Varanus tristis has been recorded raiding nests for eggs.
The termite mounds are vulnerable to damage by cattle in cattle-breeding areas, which threatens nesting sites. A flock of 28 immature birds perished after drinking water contaminated with tar at a tar spill by the side of the road.


Aviculture
The hooded parrot breeds readily in captivity but it can be aggressive, which precludes keeping it in a mixed aviary with other parrot species. It became much more popular in captivity in Australia in the 1980s. A yellow mutation has been bred.


References


Cited text
Lendon, Alan H. (1973). Australian Parrots in Field and Aviary. Angus & Robertson. ISBN 978-0-207-12424-2.


External links

BirdLife Species Factsheet

}}The '''hooded parrot''' ('''''Psephotellus dissimilis''''') is a species of parrot native to the [[Northern Territory]] in Australia. It is found in savannah and open woodland and is one of two extant species in its genus that breed in termite mounds. It has declined from much of its original range.

==Taxonomy==
One of three species known as antbed parrots, the hooded parrot is closely related to (and sometimes considered a subspecies of) the [[golden-shouldered parrot]] (''Psephotellus chrysopterygius''). A genetic study revealed its ancestors most likely diverged from ancestors of the latter species in the late Miocene or early Pliocene at the conclusion of the 'Hill Gap'.<ref name="schweizer12">{{cite journal|author1=Schweizer, Manuel |author2=Güntert, Marcel |author3=Hertwig, Stefan T. |year=2012|title=Out of the Bassian province: historical biogeography of the Australasian platycercine parrots|journal=Zoologica Scripta|volume=42|issue=1|pages=13–27|doi=10.1111/j.1463-6409.2012.00561.x|s2cid=53957317 }}</ref>

Norwegian naturalist [[Robert Collett]] described the hooded parrot in 1898. Its species name is the [[Latin]] word {{Lang|la|dissimilis}} "different" and either refers to its sexual dimorphism or its different appearance from its closest relative.<ref>{{cite book|url= https://books.google.com/books?id=z6ia8rqTa7EC&q=%22Hooded+Parrot%22&pg=PA141 | title=Australian Bird Names: A Complete Guide |author1=Gray, Jeannie |author2=Fraser, Ian |page=142 |publisher=Csiro Publishing|location=Collingwood, Victoria | isbn=978-0-643-10470-9| date=2013-05-15 }}</ref> Black-hooded parrot is an alternative name.<ref>Lendon, p. 248.</ref>

==Description==
The hooded parrot is a medium-sized [[parrot]], up to {{Convert|26|cm|abbr=on}} long, weighing {{Convert|50 to 60|g|abbr=on}}. The male has a black head, bluish-grey beak, dark-brown irises, and its wings have a yellow patch. Its upper-parts are brown, its lower parts are turquoise blue, its back is brown, the tail is olive green tipped pale blue, and its legs are pink-grey. The female is a dull-green parrot with a grey-brown head and pink undertail coverts. Juveniles are similar to the female.<ref name=Forshaw/>

The male hooded parrot can be distinguished from the similar looking male golden-shouldered parrot mainly by the absence of a pale frontal band, a larger yellow patch on the wings, and absence of the red on the abdomen. The female is very similar to the female Yellow-shouldered parrot being identified by the absence of a pale frontal band and an absence of red on the abdomen.<ref name=Forshaw>{{Cite book |first=Joseph M. |last=Forshaw |author-link=Joseph Forshaw |title=Parrots of the World; an Identification Guide |others=Illustrated by [[Frank Knight (artist)|Frank Knight]] |publisher=[[Princeton University Press]] |isbn=978-0-691-09251-5 |page=plate 58 |no-pp=yes |year=2006 |url=https://archive.org/details/parrotsofworldid0000fors }}</ref>

==Distribution and habitat==
An [[Australia]]n [[Endemism in birds|endemic]], the hooded parrot inhabits to semi-arid areas of northeast [[Northern Territory]], and is separated from the golden-shouldered parrot by the Carpentarian faunal barrier.<ref name="schweizer12"/>

Though this species has disappeared from most of its original range, it remains common in protected areas. The hooded parrot is evaluated as Least Concern on [[IUCN Red List]] of Threatened Species.<ref name="iucn" />

==Behaviour==
[[File:Hooded Parrot (Moulting Male).jpg|thumb|A hooded parrot male in moulting plumage]]
The diet consists mainly of seeds, berries and vegetables.

The hooded parrot digs cavities in [[termite]] mounds. It generally chooses conical termite mounds, only successful in flat two dimensional mounds if it digs at the edge rather than on the face of the mound. Excavation takes around three weeks.<ref name="reed94">{{cite journal|author1=Reed, Mike A. |author2=Tidemann, Sonia C. |year=1994|title=Nesting Sites of the Hooded Parrot ''Psephotus dissimilis'' in the Northern Territory|journal=Emu|volume=94|issue=4|pages=225–29|doi=10.1071/MU9940225|bibcode=1994EmuAO..94..225R }}</ref> The tunnel is roughly horizontal and around {{Convert|50 to 80|cm|abbr=on}} long.<ref name=beruldsen>{{cite book| last = Beruldsen | first = Gordon | author-link = Gordon Beruldsen | title = A Field Guide to Nests and Eggs of Australian Birds | publisher = Rigby | year = 1980 | location = Adelaide, South Australia | isbn = 978-0-7270-1202-9|page=249}}</ref>

Breeding takes place once or twice a year between April and August. The female lays four or five white, slightly shiny eggs that are {{Convert|21|mm|abbr=on}} long by {{Convert|19|mm|abbr=on}} wide.<ref name=beruldsen/>

The hooded parrot has a close ecological relationship with the [[moth]] species ''[[Trisyntopa neossophila]]''. The female moth lays eggs in the parrot nesting cavity, so that the eggs hatch at the same time as the baby hooded parrots. The moth larvae live in small tunnels at the base of the cavity and emerge to eat the dark material of parrot feces, leaving the white uric acid deposits. They build a silk layer across the base of the cavity to protect themselves from chicks. They pupate in the cavity walls once the juvenile parrots leave the nest.<ref>{{cite journal|author1=Cooney, Stuart J. N. |author2=Olsen, Penny D. |author3=Garnett, Stephen T |year=2009|title=Ecology of the coprophagous moth ''Trisyntopa neossophila'' Edwards (Lepidoptera: Oecophoridae)|journal=Australian Journal of Entomology|volume=48|issue=2|pages=97–101|doi=10.1111/j.1440-6055.2009.00691.x}}</ref>

The [[goanna]] species ''Varanus tristis'' has been recorded raiding nests for eggs.<ref name="reed94"/>[[File:Psephotus dissimilis -Northern Territory, Australia -pair-8.jpg|right|thumb|A pair in the Northern Territory, Australia]]

The termite mounds are vulnerable to damage by cattle in cattle-breeding areas, which threatens nesting sites.<ref name="reed94"/> A flock of 28 immature birds perished after drinking water contaminated with tar at a tar spill by the side of the road.<ref name=shephard/>

==Aviculture==
The hooded parrot breeds readily in captivity but it can be aggressive, which precludes keeping it in a mixed aviary with other parrot species.<ref>Lendon, p. 252.</ref> It became much more popular in captivity in Australia in the 1980s. A yellow mutation has been bred.<ref name=shephard>{{cite book |last=Shephard|first=Mark |title=Aviculture in Australia: Keeping and Breeding Aviary Birds |year=1989 |publisher=Black Cockatoo Press |location=Prahran, Victoria|pages=79–80 |isbn=978-0-9588106-0-9}}</ref>[[File:Psephotus dissimilis (female) -Burgers Zoo-8a-3c.jpg|right|thumb|Female at [[Burgers' Zoo]], Arnhem, Netherlands]]

zh.extract:
黑冠鸚鵡（學名：Psephotus dissimilis，台湾作頭巾鸚鵡），又名異色金肩鸚鵡或黃肩長尾鸚鵡，是中等身型的鸚鵡。牠們長約26厘米，呈土耳其玉色，頭部黑色，雙翼綠色，背部褐色，尾羽綠藍色，邊緣白色。牠們的喙淡灰色，腳呈灰褐色，眼睛褐色。雌鳥呈橄欖綠色，下身淡藍色。
黑冠鸚鵡是澳洲的特有種，棲息在北領地東北部半乾旱的地區。牠們會在白蟻丘上築巢。雌鳥每次會生2-4隻蛋。牠們主要吃種子、草莓及蔬菜。
雖然黑冠鸚鵡已從大部份的分佈地消失，但在一些保護地區仍很普遍。牠們被世界自然保護聯盟列為無危。
